item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't believe I never commented on this before — it's definitely on my list of favourite articles, and one of the ones that actually got me to join the site in the first place! I love this sad cube man ;=;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simply beautiful. I wish I could upvote an infinite number of times

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ALL MY FEELS

There's an art of SCP-3174 giving 2053 a "Best dad" award on Mendelssohn's art page so I came here to find out why and now I'm in a glass cage of emotion

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Doop dee doo… I'll just make sure to put the phrase 'THIS MAN TURNED YOU INTO A RUBIKS CUBE' in there… yep… permutation #24677754966450397175. There. Perfect! And I'm sure this is a good decision on my part that could never possibly bite me in the ass." - Some guy in Virginia

Also, I'd like to point out that 100% of the things the Cube could have said in the interview are already perfectly readable in the PDF/database. Get Debuggers on making portions of that shit Searchable ASAP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't sign up for this feels trip…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about the last picture really gave me the chills.

item.title: Re: +102
item.content:encoded: Looks like I may need a new profile picture.
This was a good skip. As a dad, this did hit some spots in a good way. Happy to +1 it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the best SCP I've seen about Rubik's Cube.Upvote

item.title: Depressing......
item.content:encoded: Dear Lady Gaga, this is so depressing. A father that just wishes to communicate with his son, and he can't even do that. Just goes to show how cold the Foundation can be at times…..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's messed up; a guy let himself be turned into a Rubik's Cube just to talk to his son, that he could never talk to in 9 years, and he can't even do that because he . That's like selling your soul for a cinnamon bun and then finding out it has raisins. This is so tragic it hurts. Not only that, he thinks that it worked because the Foundation lied to him, and…

Take your upvote, you tragic bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
████████, SC

If that's the city I think it is, I'll drive on down to the mental hospital a few miles away and pick him up for y'all.

In all seriousness, though, this is one of the strangest and most unique skips I've read. I don't even care that the math and computer science is a little unfeasible - it's the story that matters, and this has that level of downright oddity that makes it disturbingly compelling. I know this article is a little old, but I'm happy to have found it. Good job, dierubikdie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so sad. Ugh. I just want the Foundation to let the kid have his cube. Take your upvote and go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, "If you won't let me talk to my son, I won't let you OR HIM say anything to me!"

Actually if it was an "accident" with his mother (i.e. Daddy was a bad man!), then that attitude would fit, and Wondertainment prey'd upon it.

I like.

item.title: Re: Solving the Computer Problem
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Interestingness. Look at me, forgetting my basic cube mechanics. Jesus I can be such a derp.

Anyway, yeah, I don't know, it just seems to me like there's a difference between possibility and feasibility. If something is impossible, then, given the right impossible tools, it can be done. However, I feel like no matter what imaginable tools you have, you can't do it the way that it's presented. That's what unfeasible is. If you feel like it's perfectly fine as is, that's obviously fine, and it's a cool enough concept that it's pretty easy to look past relative nitpicks.To be honest, a part of me doesn't care and is just glad I came up with the algorithm because it's actually really cool in my opinion ^^ Anyway, thanks for the response.

item.title: Re: Solving the Computer Problem
item.content:encoded:
I think you're reading a little too far into the mathematics here. While interesting, discussing the feasibility of the document's creation is pretty much rendered pointless by the heavy implications that it was created through anomalous means. Yes, it is ridiculous to believe that a person went through and created a document containing 43 quintillion different permutations and phrases, because - as you pointed out - it's pretty damn impossible. The Foundation comes across tons of unexplained curiosities in its investigations; my intention was for the anomalous origins of this object to be one of those curiosities, not the focus of the article.

tl;dr - It doesn't have to be feasible, 'cuz it's anomalous.

Also, the permutation of the cube does exist, and is generated by the following algorithm: L' F2 L U' B2 U L' F2 L U' B2 U. Check out Waterfire's post on the first page for pictures.

item.title: Solving the Computer Problem
item.content:encoded:
What breaks my suspension of disbelief isn't the fact that somebody got that information onto a computer. What breaks it more than anything is the implication that somebody mapped 43 quintillion phrases to permutations. That's just plain ridiculous. If every person in the world mapped one per second, it would take over 170 years.

I propose a simple algorithm that forms words. Log(26^10) is about 14. Log(43 quintillion) is about 19. This means that you could EASILY fit every possible combination of letters >= 10 characters long. Modify this to only include combinations (well, permutations is the technical term but I don't want to get it mixed up with rubik's cube terminology) (well, you'd have repeat letters, so I don't know for sure to be honest, what that's called, I think there's a word for it though) including vowels, with exceptions thrown in for the very few words without vowels, and you have WAY more than enough space to fit every word and then some. Not only would this be easier, it would make a database of corresponding permutations REALLY easy to search! It would be organized by string length AND alphabetized. Even with repetition allowed, a letter would never repeat more than twice, so that could also be cut down on a lot. To create a database of permutations corresponding to strings (ignoring the problems associated with actually storing the information) would be perfectly feasible; just not the way you're doing it.

Also, nobody else has pointed it out yet, but the permutation for "WHERE IS MY SON" is impossible by turning the faces. This confuses me. Would you mind explaining? I'm sure you have a good reason.

Conceptually a great skip, though, you've got my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My intention was that the company isn't producing anomalous computers. They're just contracted by the Foundation to produce large amounts of powerful computers, capable of handling the necessary computing load.

Also, in my head, that company is
Also, in my head, that company is Google.

EDIT: It's been so long since I've visited this thread, I forgot that I had already sort of mentioned this higher up in the thread. Oops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
@Voct
huh, ur right, looking back i dont remember where i got that number from, still, that would just make the scale of the database even more ridiculous.

@pro-mole
Idk, i think ur definition of "feasible" may be a little different from mine. The foundation's resources are near-infinite but not omnipotent. As I said, just searching an array of that size would take hours for a modern supercomputer. For the foundation to get a response time reasonable enough to hold an extended conversation would require the power of hundreds of supercomputers. At that point we may have to start worrying about the amount of resources left on Earth let alone the foundation.

@dierubikdie
I guess u can explain away everything with anomalous computers. Though i feel like a computer capable of doing these things would be an SCP itself. It also seems a little out of character for the foundation to be collaborating with a company that's in the business of actively manufacturing anomalous objects.

item.title: Sentimental...
item.content:encoded:
Sentimental, but it works for me. I understand the computer science issues, but they trouble me not at all - Foundation super computers, and so on. This is short and to the point, as it should be. Trying to extend the backstory to discover more about these people, beyond the situation as it's presented, would ruin the tight article structure. SCP articles are mostly snapshots, not novels.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since 43 Quintilion is roughly 2^11

Very roughly. 2^11 is 2048.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think less would be more. Everything past the first interview is unnecessary.

The last half of dialogue was a bit uninspired and could probably be condensed.

SCP-2053-1: I MET A GUY WHO SAID HE COULD GET JAKE TO TALK

SCP-2053-1: HE KNEW A DOCTOR IN VIRGINIA OR SOMETHING WHO COULD FIX JAKE

SCP-2053-1: I JUST WANTED TO TALK TO MY SON

Dr. Boone: THIS MAN TURNED YOU INTO A RUBIKS CUBE

SCP-2053-1: IT WORKED DIDNT IT I GOT TO TALK TO MY SON AFTER NINE YEARS OF NOTHING

eyeroll

Doctor turns people into rubiks cubes? Okay. Last picture is oddly creepy though.

As a whole this is pretty good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, aside from the incredibly specific database of sentences and the fact that I can't for the love of me understand why the Foundation won't seize Jake and get him in touch with this cube — monitor closely and there should be no problem; at least it's likely that they'll be able to learn something of the past of the cube if it's not clammed in one immutable position — I dig this story.

Oh, yes, and someone gotta investigate about this doctor that turns people into rubik's cubes. Last time I checked that wasn't a thing doctors were qualified to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a degree in CS as well so I too understand how impossible the whole thing is. If anything, the compression format in that file is just insane, and no commercial software should be able to open it as a normal PDF(barring, of course, computer magic). Hell, no software would even be able to display that number of pages, and by that I mean the number itself.

A database, though, would be… feasible. I mean, the Foundation has nearly infinite resources, so cooking up an image recognition system with a few cameras, some normalization to align the faces according to the diagrams and then index the whole universe of combinations and even spit back the permutations needed to communicate back to it is not totally impossible. It's just incredibly impractical and may need a few million processors running in parallel, a hell of a cooling system to keep whatever room they're kept in from triggering nuclear fusion and several million interns to convert all those goddamn PDF pages into a database that can actually be searched.

Because, really, searching patterns in a randomly sorted PDF file? Now that would be just too much. :P

But yes, computer magic. At least it's sound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit, some of the things you mentioned did cross my mind while writing this; my solution was to imply that the tools used to communicate with the object are also somehow anomalous:

Using computing assets obtained from ██████ Inc., personnel assigned to SCP-2053 have created a digital database containing all of these diagrams and their corresponding phrases.

I didn't explicitly state that these computing assets were anomalous, but I wanted the blackboxes here to imply that the Foundation may have seized them because they were anomalous.

Fun little side note...
Fun little side note...

In my headcanon, the company's name? Google.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry but the computer science involved here is simply too impractical to be believed.

First of all, building a database of this pdf file. The article itself admits that an actual pdf file with 43 quintillion pages would be impossibly large, why would a (non-anomalous) database built on the information in such a file be an different? To be precise: a rubik's cube has 43 quintillion combinations. Since 43 Quintilion is roughly 2^11, that means we need at least 11 bits or more than 1 byte of information to store each combination. 43 Quintillion data structures, each of which is 11 bits in size would lead to a total size of several exabytes. This is already 6 orders of magnitude greater than any hard drive/storage center owned by ANY organization in existence today. On top of that I havn't even accounted for the amount of space required to store the corresponding English phrases which would multiply the size several times(using standard encoding each individual character would take up a byte).

Secondly, actually SEARCHING such a database would be a nightmare. Assume for simplicity that looking at a single combination in the database and checking it against input only takes as much time as a single floating-point operation(in reality it would probably take much longer). Even a modern supercomputer clocking in at several petaflops would take hours to look through 43 quintillion permutations.

Maybe my background in CS is making it hard to suspend disbelief but for me, this is just too outrageous of a scale to be believable unless the computer they're using for this is anomalous in and of itself.

There is however, a way around this, if you're willing to make a slight change. If the sequence of rubiks premutation in the pdf file is sorted that is, it follows some type of regular, predictable pattern, it should then be possible to make a function that computes just from looking at a permutation, the exact page number corresponding to that particular permutation. This could then be used to search the pdf itself in fast time.

edit: actually, even under this scheme, searching for phrases would still be next to impossible

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man. This is so unique. And sad. +1.

item.title: Re: JAKE. PICK THE CUBE JAKE. PLEASE.
item.content:encoded:
Note that 43252003274489856000 > 264, so a realistic PDF reader would overflow on the page count.

The article does mention custom software though, so perhaps they got around this limitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plus, there are far, far fewer than 43 quintillion words in English. I don't see anything in the skip that prohibits chaining words or phrases together in sequence. By simply including every word in the database, you can construct any sentence. After you've inuded all the English words, you can devote the rest of the database to phrases in order of commonality, allowing you to cut down on the time it takes to say something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is amazing! But dad should have just hooked up SCP-603 if he wanted to talk to Jacob.

item.title: Re: JAKE. PICK THE CUBE JAKE. PLEASE.
item.content:encoded:
IIRC the PDF format is turing complete, so it could conceivably generate the pages "on the fly" while still being relatively small. It could generate a page for each permutation, and have a function that maps the permutation's number to a string of characters.

According to my calculation, if each character in the string can be one of 30 characters (a-z.?!_) then the PDF file would contain all possible strings of length 13 or less. "WHERE IS MY SON" is a little over 13, but if the function is slightly optimized to only map to "english looking" sentences then the rubik's cube could probably say a lot longer things.

So it's entirely possible that the PDF is not anomalous, just very, very hacky. Reminds me of those ZIP bombs that are ~1k compressed, but 4 GB uncompressed. It also puts into perspective just how many permutations a rubik's cube has. Insane
The world will know the feels.
SOMEONE forgot about +100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it. I definitely wanted more from the interviews in the end, but that didn't stop me from upvoting by any means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow, I just noticed that this article was translated and posted to the Russian site! Thanks, Gene R!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very absorbing, with its own quiet tragedy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Blame isn't relevant ashausesall, don't worry about it. I hadn't even seen Zyn's post above, so I came here wondering who were disagreeing, only to find out it was me. ;)

In the end it was just a misunderstanding and that can and will happen. It's all good. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry to be offensive. I don't want to teach anybody. Never.
In the beginning I just didn't think it's fair to give judgement without careful reading….Well, I should have know that once we involved in this SCP writing it's fair to be downvoted/upvoted by any reason. I should be careful before making comments. It's my fault. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually my second mainlist article, but it was the first idea I had when I joined the site! Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made me sad and wanna upvote

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In me, the feel eel feels eel feels.

But seriously, what a concept for your first skip. There are some timmi and Eric overtones here and there, but nothing that would cloud it overall. You pretty much had me at the image, same as Laugh is life. I just had to read this, finding it good and simple.

I also really like the connection of your profile pic, nickname and your first skip.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ashausesall, Crayne has already stated that he did not feel sad after reading the article. If he disagrees with you, you don't need to explain your point. You are fine having your own opinion, just like he is. Each person is allowed to vote the way they want to, and people are free to disagree with that. Nobody needs to defend why they voted the way they did.

ashausesall, it's fine for you to believe that people should vote carefully after looking at every detail. However, that does not mean that everyone needs to be that way.

Crayne, I think you might have misinterpreted a bit; it looks like ashausesall was just voicing an opinion about how he thought people should vote, not necessarily implying that you needed to do so the same way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Katie, please leave this sort of communication to staff, please. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My logic: 43 quintillion is a lot, but probably not enough to include all possible English sentences. However, if you limit yourself to a basic vocabulary of a few hundred words or so (including a few important words like "Jake" and "progressive mutism"), then that number becomes sufficient. For example, you wouldn't be able to say complicated phrases like "A molecule is achiral (not chiral) when an improper rotation, that is a combination of a rotation and a reflection in a plane, perpendicular to the axis of rotation, results in the same molecule." But you could say "hi Jake how was your day did you do well on your algebra test".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one a lot. The pictures really did it for me. I just have one issue that maybe someone with a better grasp of very large numbers could help me with. It seems highly unlikely to me that the cube would have been essentially preprogrammed with the phrases " KNEW A DOCTOR IN VIRGINIA OR SOMETHING WHO COULD FIX JAKE" and especially "IT WORKED DIDNT IT I GOT TO TALK TO MY SON AFTER NINE YEARS OF NOTHING," which has the quality of both being a response (which couldn't stand alone as a statement) and being completely time-dependent (can only be used nine years later and only if exactly eight years have passed). Is 43 quintillion enough to make the inclusion of these phrases probable? Either way, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't want to take this too far away from the article at hand, but I just wanted to ask if you might be from an Asian country? (I see a distinct cultural difference between the way you and Crayne are communicating that feels very familiar to me from tutoring Asian international students. Knowing if this is the case might help others to better understand your intentions and might help you to better understand how we talk on this site and why we do it that way. That's all.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good, I love it, apart from the ending. I think it makes it way too obvious. When you got to the part where they found Jake, I though there was going to be some mindfuck where the researcher is actually talking to himself in some way through the cube.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, he IS picking his responses from a list, a very long list that's difficult to search. It's entirely possible that better phrasings either weren't in there, or were unable to be found.

This article has a palpable aura of desperation about it in the father's desperate and doomed attempt to reconnect with his son and tell him he's proud. I wonder if the doctor himself might be feeling regret that he accidentally let the guy believe he was talking to his son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good. The story is cool and the writing is solid. However, the way that the doctor talks to the father in the second log strikes me as somewhat unrealistically inexpert. Seemed like he was wording things in such a way as to put the father under unnecessary duress, which doesn't make all that much sense for the organization, as at that point they were aware of the situation and the father's motivations, and as such should have been able to predict his negative response to such an approach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not an English user so there is some wrong spelling. Sorry.
Nobody can teach others how to make judgement on SCP, and it's alright to down or up for any reason you like. But I think we should respect the author and his work by giving down or up carefully.
Maybe I think too much,but here most of the posts say noting about the idea. All I want to do is giving advise, not telling people what they should do.
I mean,why not read it carefully and try to find every amazing point in it? I'm doing some translating work so I have to read this item word by word,and I have to say it is cool. Of cause for you it's not cool enough, but at least we have already read it carefully and give the author our respect, right? Down orup is up to you, but I really think we should do it carefully.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly have problems understanding what exactly it is you're trying to say here. Please pay attention to your grammar and spelling.

But yes, downvoting an article based on its failure to move the reader is valid. I'm glad you liked, but it did nothing for me.

Please don't tell others why they should or should not vote the way they do, not even for argument's sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don‘t think so. They only "should" in items is to give the reader a good feeling. Moving and amazing are both good feeling.
You can't say "It's good,very good; but from my point of view the guy seems trying to make me cry but I don't. So I have to down it for this, although it's really good." I think we should judge by yourself not the author. Maybe he failed to make us moving but a good idea has nothing with that.
Of cause maybe you don't think the idea it used is good enough. But I think it is and I don't care it is moving or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not every good item makes you tearing or moving, right？

No, but items specially trying to elicit empathy or sadness should.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don’t think sadness is the only point. It's good but the idea used in this SCP(turned someone into a cube and talked with picture)makes me upvote. It's really a cool idea.
If you say "this is not sad or moving enough and I'll down it for this". I think you may not catch the point or ignore something. Not every good item makes you tearing or moving, right？

item.title: Re: JAKE. PICK THE CUBE JAKE. PLEASE.
item.content:encoded: Nah. That'd probably make it all happy and shit.

item.title: Eh.
item.content:encoded: It's an alright SCP, nothing too new or special. I honestly don't understand how this is in any way sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My eyes
They are wet

[something about the all caps conversation really hit me hard]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My new motto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: no way really? and which one of them do you use for your picture?
